coredata.dc:title: Multiple color emission of solid-state hybrid material containing carbon dots and Europium(III) complexes
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bo
Yang, Jing
Li, Huanrong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, multiple color emitting solid-state carbon dots with europium (III) complexes hybrid material has been prepared through in situ synthesis within the confined interlayer of the host clay. The emission color of the resulting hybrid material can be tuned from red to blue through changing the excitation wavelength, where the white emission (CIE-(0.27, 0.28)) can be achieved under 302 nm UV light excitation. Therefore, the as-prepared material possesses a promising prospect in the area of optics, especially in white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Europium (III) complexes
Hybrid material
Color-tunable
Solid-state
White light

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the luminescent hybrid material CDs@Lap
Preparation of the luminescent hybrid material Eu-CDs@Lap
The procedure of etching the lap with hydrochloric acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The XRD spectra of pure Lap and CDs@Lap, (b) FT-IR spectra of pure Lap and CDs@Lap, (c)TEM image of the CDs (inset showing the particle size distribution, (d) HRTEM image of the particles.

(a) Optical images of Eu-CDs@Lap prepared under different wavelength UV lamp illumination, (b) the CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates of the white light, (c) the XPS pattern of the Eu-CDs@Lap, (d) the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of Eu-CDs@Lap.

(a), (c), (e) The excitation spectra of material monitored by emission at 615 nm, 480 nm, 615 nm, respectively. (b), (d), (f) The emission spectra of the material upon excitation at 283 nm, 385 nm, 302 nm, respectively.

(a), (b)The decay curves of Eu3+ in Eu-CDs@Lap and EuCl3-CDs@Lap under 283 nm excitation wavelength, respectively. (c), (d)The decay curves of CDs in Eu-CDs@Lap and CDs@Lap under 385 nm UV illumination, respectively. (e), (f) The decay curves of CDs and Eu3+ in Eu-CDs@Lap upon excitation with 302 nm, respectively.

head.ce:title: Multiple color emission of solid-state hybrid material containing carbon dots and Europium(III) complexes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology Hebei University of Technology, GuangRong Dao 8, Hongqiao Distric, Tianjin, 300130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology Hebei University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology Hebei University of Technology, GuangRong Dao 8, Hongqiao Distric, Tianjin, 300130, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, multiple color emitting solid-state carbon dots with europium (III) complexes hybrid material has been prepared through in situ synthesis within the confined interlayer of the host clay. The emission color of the resulting hybrid material can be tuned from red to blue through changing the excitation wavelength, where the white emission (CIE-(0.27, 0.28)) can be achieved under 302 nm UV light excitation. Therefore, the as-prepared material possesses a promising prospect in the area of optics, especially in white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs).

Multiple color emitting solid-state hybrid material was prepared by embedding carbon dots and Eu(DPA)3 into the host clay, where the emission color, including red, white and blue, can be tuned by changing excitation wavelength.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively investigated for decades owing to their advantages such as good optical properties, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and facile preparation [1–5]. Among the advantages, optical properties increasingly attract the attention of researchers [6–9]. However, nowadays, the majority of CDs show emission color in the blue-to-green spectral region, which restricts their application in multiple-color optical devices [6,10–12]. In order to overcome this problem, CDs can be used to combine with trivalent lanthanide ions like Europium (III) (Eu3+), which may develop luminescent organic-inorganic hybrid materials endowing with multiple color emission. The choice of trivalent lanthanide ions has been driven by their unique light emission properties, such as sharp emission profiles and long-lived excited states [13,14]. The emission intensities of bare lanthanide ions are weak due to the forbidden nature of the f-f transitions, which, fortunately, can be overcome by the so-called antenna effect (or sensitization) using appropriate organic ligand molecules and resulting in lanthanide complexes featured with distinctive luminescence properties [15,16]. The above strategy to obtain lanthanide doped CDs has been demonstrated by Chen and Kailasa, to name just a few [17–20], but most of them suffer from fluorescence-quenching in solid state.

Encapsulation of luminescent CDs in matrices such as polymers and silica gel to prepare the solid-state luminescent hybrid materials has been fascinating researchers recently, since they prevent CDs from fluorescence-quenching in solid state in order to improve the practical application value in solid state devices like light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and optoelectronic devices [11,21,22]. We have engaged in the development of solid-state luminescent CDs by enlarging the distance among CDs particles using 2D layered structure-nanoclay (Lap) due to its advantages such as good adsorption property and adjustable layer spacing [23,24]. However, the above visual color emission of solid-state CDs reported by us are lack of red emission, therefore we can combine CDs and Europium (III) complexes [11]. For further improving the practical application, herein we report a novel color-tunable solid-state luminescent material (Eu-CDs@Lap) developed via one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid (CA), urea and Eu(DPA)3 and Lap, where Lap is expected to disperse CDs in order to make the obtained materials luminescent in the solid state. The one-pot hydrothermal method we adopted is easily operated and environment-friendly due to using double distilled water as the reaction solvent [25]. The obtained hybrid materials display tunable emission colors from red to blue through variation of excitation wavelength where the Eu3+ and CDs mainly offers the red and blue emission, respectively. Interestingly, Eu-CDs@Lap can emit white light so that it can be potential candidate for white light emitting diodes (WLEDs).

Urea (99%), citric acid (CA, 98%), EuCl3·6H2O (99.9%), 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA, 99%), NaHCO3(99%), alcohol (99.7%), hydrochloric acid (99%), double distilled water and the clay Laponite (Lap) were all used as purchased, without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the luminescent hybrid material CDs@Lap
ce:section.ce:para: 0.2 g of Lap was dissolved in 10 mL double distilled water to form transparent hydrosol, then 0.25 mmol CA and 0.75 mmol urea were added into the hydrosol. After stirring the mixture for 1 h, the mixture was transferred into 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated to 240 °C, and kept for 6 h. The mixture was centrifuged for 10 min to remove the supernatant, and then it was dried for 48 h at the atmosphere of vacuum and followed by grind. The material CDs@Lap was finally acquired.

0.25 mmol CA and 0.75 mmol urea were mixed into 10 mL double distilled water. After stirring for 1 h, the mixture was transferred into 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated at 240 °C for 6 h. The mixture was centrifuged to remove the supernatant, and then it was dried for 12 h at the atmosphere of vacuum. The CDs powder was acquired for measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the luminescent hybrid material Eu-CDs@Lap
ce:section.ce:para: Eu(DPA)3 was first synthesized by stirring the mixture of DPA and EuCl3 (the molar ratio = 3:1) for 2 h and using NaHCO3 to make the solvent neutral" should be"Eu(DPA)3 was first synthesized by stirring the mixture of DPA and EuCl3 (the molar ratio = 3:1) for 2 h and using NaHCO3 to make the solvent neutral and following solvent evaporation.

0.2 g of Lap was dissolved in 10 mL double distilled water to form transparent hydrosol, then 0.15 mmol EuCl3, 0.25 mmol CA and 0.75 mmol urea were added into the hydrosol, and then the material EuCl3-CDs@Lap was obtained after the similar process to the synthesis of CDs@Lap.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The procedure of etching the lap with hydrochloric acid
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 g CDs@Lap was dispersed in 50 mL HCl (1 mol/L) and stirred for 24 h, then purified with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to get rid of the Lap. The filtrate was collected for further characterizing.

The X-ray powder diffraction data (XRD) was collected on a Bruker D8 diffractometer complying Cu Kα radiation. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) powder spectra of the samples was obtained on a Bruker Vector 22 spectrometer using KBr pellets for the powder samples. The luminescence (excitation and emission) spectra and the lifetimes were measured on an Edinburgh Instrument FS920P spectrometer, with a 450 W xenon lamp as the steady-state excitation source, a double excitation monochromator (1800 lines mm−1), an emission monochromator (600 lines mm-1) and a semiconductor cooled Hamamatsu RMP928 photo multipliertube. The absolute luminescence quantum yields were calculated on the Edinburgh Instruments FLS920P. The X-ray photoelectron data was obtained by a ESCALAB 250Xi x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS).TEM images were achieved by a FEI Tecnai G2F20 transmission electron microscopy.

The solid-state hybrid material CDs@Lap was synthesized through a facial hydrothermal reaction of moderate CA and urea containing a suitable amount of Lap, where CA serves as a carbon source and urea as a nitrogen source and the host Lap provides confined space to avoid aggregation and make the CDs@Lap luminescent in solid state by nano-confinement. As reported [11], the intercalation of guest molecules in Lap interlayers usually causes the enlargement of the interlayers. We therefore characterized the XRD pattern of the samples and the data are shown in Fig. 1 a. The d spacing of Lap enlarges after the accommodation of CDs, proving the successful intercalation of the guest CDs into the interlayer space of the clay and further making the solid-state CDs@Lap avoid aggregation-induced fluorescence. Obviously, the other diffraction peaks at their corresponding diffraction angles has not changed, indicating that there is no obvious changes in the structure of the clay during the hydrothermal reaction. Therefore, the solid-state hybrid materials are expected to be highly luminescent under UV light. As shown in Fig. S1, the solid-state CDs@Lap emits blue light under UV light. Furthermore, FT-IR and XPS spectra were applied to measure the function groups of CDs@Lap. As is displayed in Fig. 1b, the FT-IR spectrum contains peaks at 457, 655, 1014, 1630 and 3700 cm−1 contributed to Lap. Then the other peaks displaying in the figure of CDs@Lap belong to the CDs: the peak at 1570 cm−1 is owing to the bending vibration of N–H [26], and the 1716 cm−1 peak is originated from the carbonyl(CO) from COOH groups [27]. In addition, the peaks at 3431 and 1415 cm−1 derives from the stretching vibration and bending vibration of Ο-H, respectively, and the 1670 cm−1 band is due to the vibration of CO [28]. The above results show there are the functional groups –COOH, –OH, –NH2 and –CO–NH- on the surface of the CDs. In addition, we have measured the function groups of pure CDs by FT-IR, and it also confirms the existence of these functional groups (see Fig. S2). The XPS of the sample further confirms the above FT-IR analysis. The material contains the element of C, N and O shown in the XPS survey (Fig. S3a), and it can be implied that it contains the same chemical bonds by analyzing their deconvoluted peaks (Fig. S3b). Afterwards, the Lap of CDs@Lap was etched by hydrochloric acid in order to study the size and morphology of CDs by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). According to the TEM image of CDs (Fig. 1c), the CDs particles are well-dispersed and the average size of CDs is about 4 nm. The corresponding high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (Fig. 1d) shows a lattice spacing distance of 0.21 nm, which agreed with that of graphite (100) facet [29–32].

However, as reported [33,34], most synthesized solid state CDs were unable to emit red emission including the above CDs@Lap prepared by us, which limits their practical application to a large extent. Herein, we embedded the Eu(DPA)3, which is a favorable red luminescent material due to their unique features including large Stokes shifts, tunable emission bands and long-lived excited states [35–37], into the Lap at the same time to coordinate with CDs, the resulting material is named Eu-CDs@Lap. As shown in the XPS spectrum of Eu-CDs@Lap (Fig. 2 c), Eu-CDs@Lap contains element of C, N, O and Eu, and the high-resolution XPS pattern of C1s shows that the dopant of Eu3+ does not influence the chemical bands of the material. In addition, the mapping analysis also reveals that the material possesses the element of C, N, O and Eu (Fig. S4). These results illustrate that Eu3+ ions have been introduced into the above CDs@Lap successfully, leading that Eu-CDs@Lap may emit red emission.

Then, we investigated the optical properties of Eu-CDs@Lap. Interestingly, the resulting hybrid material emits different colors of light under UV light. In detail, the material exhibits red, blue and white light, when they are under 254 nm, 365 nm and 302 nm excitation wavelength, respectively (Fig. 2a and b). The UV–vis spectrum of Eu-CDs@Lap (Fig. S5) shows absorption bands at 264 nm and 286 nm due to the transition of π→π* of CC and n→π* transition of CO in the material, respectively [11,38], and it also shows absorption peak at 272 nm resulting from Eu(DPA)3 [37], so there may be a part of DPA in the material not carbonized. The excitation spectrum shown in Fig. 3 a monitored by 615 nm exhibits a broad band ranging from 250 to 300 nm with the maximum excitation wavelength of 283 nm contributed to the absorption of DPA ligand [39]. Excitation at 283 nm leads to line-like emission bands arising from the transition of 5D0→7F J (J = 0,1, 2, 3 and 4) peaking at 579, 590, 615, 649 and 693 nm, respectively, among which the 5D0→7F1 transition of the emission peak at 615 nm serves as the prominent one for the bright red luminescence (Fig. 3b) [40]. The luminescent quantum yield (QY) at 283 nm excitation in Eu-CDs@Lap is 10.3%, and the corresponding lifetime is 0.4 ms according to Fig. 4 a. Moreover, in the emission spectrum, a weak peaks at 480 nm attributed to CDs exists corresponding the broad excitation band from 300 nm to 360 nm in the excitation spectrum, indicating that Eu3+ is the main emitter while the luminescence of CDs are very weak under excitation at 283 nm. Afterwards, as shown in Fig. 3c, the excitation spectrum of Eu-CDs@Lap monitored by 480 nm is also measured, consisting of a broad band ranging from 300 to 470 nm with peaks at 385 nm. In the emission spectrum, the emission peaks at 480 nm are dominant while the emission peaks at 592 and 615 nm are weak (see Fig. 3d), and the material (Eu-CDs@Lap) displays blue emission color under 385 nm UV light illumination. As previous analysis, we can conclude that the peaks at 592 and 615 nm originate from Eu3+ while the emission peaks at 480 nm belongs to CDs, showing that the emission intensity of CDs is much stronger than that of Eu3+. The luminescent QY of CDs at 385 nm excitation is 7%, and the lifetime of CDs is about 1.1 ns in Eu-CDs@Lap (shown as Fig. 4c). Therefore, there are two luminous centers in Eu-CDs@Lap, which can be further confirmed by the 2D excitation-emission spectrum (Fig. S6). Moreover, the synthesized material exhibits excitation dependent emission spectral properties as shown in Fig. S7. The presence of red emission (Eu3+) and emission in blue-green region (CDs) in the hybrid material thus offer us with opportunity to gain white-light emission through varying excitation wavelength. For example, the excitation peaks of CDs and Eu(DPA)3 both appear under 302 nm excitation wavelength shown in Fig. 3e, we can easily obtain white-light emission of Eu-CDs@Lap excited at 302 nm due to the close emission intensity of Eu3+ and CDs (see Fig. 3f). Under this excitation wavelength, the luminescent QY of hybrid material is 8.7%, and we measured that the lifetime of CDs is 0.76 ns and the lifetime value of Eu3+ is 0.44 ms according to Fig. 4e and f. These properties make it potential candidates in optical devices, especially for white LED devices.

Why is herein Eu(DPA)3 rather than EuCl3 introduced into Lap? Because when Eu(DPA)3 is replaced by EuCl3 (named as EuCl3-CDs@Lap), the red luminescence of which is much weaker compared to Eu-CDs@Lap (shown as Figs. S8 and S9). As shown in Fig. 4b, the lifetime of Eu3+ is 0.47 ms at 283 nm excitation in EuCl3-CDs@Lap, and the absolute PL quantum yield (QY) values of Eu3+ is just 0.9%, which is very lower compared the Eu3+ in Eu-CDs@Lap. What is more, there is no absorption band of DPA in the excitation spectrum of EuCl3-CDs@Lap monitored by emission at 615 nm (see Fig. S10), while there is an absorption band corresponding to DPA in the excitation spectrum of Eu-CDs@Lap (Fig. 3a), indicating that the bright red luminescence of Eu3+ is due to the sensitization of DPA to Eu3+ in Eu-CDs@Lap. Hence, the ligand DPA is essential to add to sensitize Eu3+. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 3c and d, there is no excitation bands of DPA but the excitation bands of CDs in the 385 nm excitation spectrum, so the appearance of emission peaks of Eu3+ illustrates that CDs could sensitize the Eu3+. The quantum yield of CDs in CDs@Lap is 8.3% under 385 nm excitation, which is higher than that of Eu-CDs@Lap possibly due to the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ in Eu-CDs@Lap. In contrast, the quantum yield of Eu3+ is about 9.5% without CDs, which is lower than that of Eu-CDs@Lap, therefore, the luminescent intensity of Eu3+ was enhanced when CDs exist [41]. To further investigate the sensitization effect of CDs on Eu3+, the lifetimes of CDs in Eu-CDs@Lap and CDs@Lap were measured, as shown in Fig. 4c and d. The lifetime of CDs decreases from 1.7 ns to 1.1 ns when Eu3+ exists, further implying the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+, which can be further explored.

In summary, we have demonstrated an easy hydrothermal method to obtain the solid-state multi-color luminescent hybrid material (Eu-CDs@Lap) by embedding lanthanide complexes Eu(DPA)3 and CDs into the Lap. The emission colors including red, white and blue of Eu-CDs@Lap can be tuned by changing excitation wavelength from 283 nm to 385 nm, where the red and blue color are owed to Eu3+ and CDs, respectively. These luminescent characters make the hybrid material potential candidates for the visual multiple color light emitting devices, especially WLEDs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2019.116959.
Contributors: Bo Zhang conducted the experiments, Bo Zhang and Jing Yang analyzed the experimental data and drafted the manuscript. Huanrong Li supervised the work and edited the manuscript.

This manuscript is being exclusively submitted to Journal of Luminescence, and not currently under consideration for publication elsewhere. No related work, not cited in the manuscript, is under consideration for publication or has been published elsewhere in any medium. We have read and understood your journal's policies, and we believe that neither the manuscript nor the study violates any of these. There are no conflicts of interest to declare. All authors have approved the submission of the manuscript.
This work is financially supported by the Graduate outstanding innovative topics of Hebei Province (220056).
